title: Enhancing the resilience of the gilt repo market – discussion paper feedback statement
summary: The Bank of England has published a feedback statement summarizing industry responses to its September 2025 discussion paper on gilt repo market resilience, outlining potential reforms including greater central clearing and minimum haircuts on non-centrally cleared transactions. Respondents broadly supported the resilience objective but raised concerns about proportionality, access barriers, and potential negative spillovers from market-wide mandates, favouring incentives for voluntary adoption over mandatory requirements.
key_points: The Bank cites two historical stress episodes – the March 2020 'dash for cash' and the September 2022 LDI episode – as motivation for strengthening gilt repo market resilience.
Respondents identified existing barriers to central clearing including access constraints, operational limitations, and cost considerations that make it uneconomical for most participants.
Most respondents opposed mandatory central clearing and non-risk-sensitive minimum haircuts, expressing concerns these would raise funding costs and divert activity to other markets.
Proposed incentives for voluntary clearing adoption include cross-product margining, expanded acceptable collateral pools, and clearing of the Bank's Short-Term Repo facility.
Some respondents suggested measures including innovations to the Contingent Non-Bank Financial Institutions Repo Facility (CNRF), less procyclical CCP margin models, and tri-party repo development.
The Bank commits to exploring market structure changes that would enable greater central clearing adoption and identifying how obstacles to adoption can be overcome.
why_it_matters: This feedback statement signals the Bank of England's policy direction for UK repo market infrastructure reforms. Market participants who operate in gilt repo – including hedge funds, dealers, and institutional investors – need to understand that the Bank is committed to structural reforms despite industry concerns about implementation costs and operational complexity. Changes to clearing mandates or haircut requirements could affect funding costs and liquidity provision in the gilt market.
market_structure_context: The document directly addresses structural reforms to the UK gilt repo market, including central clearing adoption, haircut requirements, CCP margin procyclicality, dealer balance sheet constraints, and settlement infrastructure improvements. The Bank is working with the FCA, HM Treasury, and DMO on these reforms.
notes: Source is a high-quality Bank of England policy consultation response. Published April 1, 2026 – within 90 days of TODAY, so not stale. Documents industry feedback and Bank's intended direction on gilt repo market structural reforms. Source body was truncated but sufficient content was available for analysis.
